item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that the first image is cited from Pinterest as being from here, which states permission must be obtained before usage, which has not been stated here. As such, the image has been removed.

Due to the specificity of its content, the second image is assumed to be user-created and thus compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm impressed . That is all. + 1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the diagram could use a little clearing up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject was a suspected collaborator with Group of Interest Marshall, Carter & Dark. Two other surplus samples were created from this subject but were since destroyed.

Does this mean The Body, Mr Marshall! is on its way?

People don't forget the promises you make, SRegan! People don't forget!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humanity never existed. Neither did the teapot. We are all just star-eating slugs, experiencing sitting at our computers, reading a story on the Internet about ourselves drinking tea to experience our other lives. And actually there is only one slug, and we are all She.

I still don't feel any existential dread, but maybe that's just this version of me. anqxyr slithers away to the nearest tasty star.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also to take this one step further, let's say some future version of MC&D or The Foundation figures out how to synthesize and modify the liquid. What if they could build entirely fictional experiences? You couldn't even know then that someone had at some point lived that experience, the only thing you have proof of at that point is that there is an experiencer of some type. Think of the commercial applications - instead of taking a vacation, simply have the memories of a great vacation (or a great adventure) directly implanted in your mind. Be a superspy! Save mars!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My read of it is in line with your second scenario. You are not experiencing the sampled person as an outside observer. By "experiencing" they mean that you become the other person while in the replay. It's not a freaky friday situation where your consciousness is inserted into another body or a being john malkovich situation where you are doing a ride-along. You are them. You make all the same choices they would because you are the same person in the same situation. You would have the illusion of free will but would do all the same things because there is no difference. Even using the word "you" here is misleading because it implies a separate consciousness. There is only one, and you're riding their rails.

In a universe where this substance exists, you would not know that you exist. You might know that at some point someone historically existed that had the experiences you are currently inhabiting, but there's no way to know if you do actually exist currently. I think therefore I am doesn't apply when we can no longer know that you are "I".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Inner_Light_%28Star_Trek:_The_Next_Generation%29

But with the implications explored to the point that it becomes creepy.

Well done. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, that was pretty much my thinking - the psychological interrogation she's been through is a significant factor in her feelings about the skip.

There are legitimate philosophical implications if you know that there is a non-zero possibility that you are experiencing an illusion; the matrix/brain in a jar argument's weakness is that there is no reason to believe it to be true.

As an example, suppose you arrange that when you die your body will be cut up into hundreds of pieces and used to prepare a large amount of tea, to be administered to x people. It's actually a version of the Carter Catastrophe - assuming a non-privileged observer (the observer in this case is perfectly unprivileged as drinkers have no memory of their real life during the experience), your chances of being the original you are 1/x. In this way 'you' could in theory live forever, passing your memories and experiences in an infinite chain up into the future, where the ultimate drinker and thus your 'real' identity remains undefined. Arguably none of the 'yous' in the chain would be acting rationally because each would act as though they personally were going to continue living at the point of death, but the sum of their experiences would appear to be a single immortal individual passing from life to life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On a tangent, who here has read Witches Abroad?
I think Granny Weatherwax sums up the proposition quite succinctly near the end of that story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and this existence actually did occur independently at some point.

Furthermore, if you're experiencing a replay, you essentially don't have free will. Any actions you take or thoughts you think are that of the original. If the original was acting on the assumption that his life is a lie and he is actually some other person… well, I'd say that there's something seriously wrong with his head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I don't have that existential horror thing, either. There are two possibilities: either the current perception of experiences is in time-sync with external reality, in which case I'm me experiencing my own existence, or the current perception of experiences if NOT in time-sync, but rather a perfect replay that the external observer is unable to distinguish from exact personal experiences, up to and including internal realities and the suppression of external thoughts, in which case I'm still me experiencing my own existence, just… nested. All I can do is live my life in accordance with the assumption that I'm NOT a replay, because there's literally no way to determine that from "inside" the replay, and this existence actually did occur independently at some point. And since it did occur at some point, why not act as if this is the original occurrence, rather than a replay.

In other words: it's hard for me to have existential doubt because I know that I exist in and of myself. Exterior meanings or events are unrelated to that knowledge.

ANYWAY, I do like the article, even if I'm not as deeply unsettled by it as was the intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that a lot.
It's not exactly the tea's effects that are causing her issues, but rather the combination of effect and related trauma.
I think I'll steal that for my own head-canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm with you on that one. And it's not even like the nested lives are simulations of some sort that would imply theological connotations and shit. They're all real people, both the ones who experience the lifetime and the ones whose lifetime is experienced. Some of them just happen to have extensive memories belonging to another person.

That said, I still like the article, and I'm willing to attribute Chen's overreaction to the backwash of the interrogation or some prior existential insecurities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just don't grok why such an uncertainty would be anything but mildly unsettling or curious. Can you say, right now IRL, that you are not a butterfly dreaming you are a man? But we (most of us, anyway) don't live in fear of some "real" us waking up and finding out everything we think we are is really a delusion. On the flip side, when we wake up from even the most realistic dream, it is not horrifying to realize that we can not really fly or whatever the dream-us was doing.

You are, in a very real sense, the person you are experiencing at any given time. If it turns out later that you were objectively wrong at that moment, it doesn't matter because now you are the person who was "really" experiencing the delusion. Repeat the inception layers as many times as you like, and it only strikes me as disorienting, not horrific.

It's entirely possible I am just not psychologically wired in a way to grasp this. It wouldn't be the first time, not even the first time on this wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Person C experiences life of Person B experiencing the life of Person A. Person B isn't uncertain whether he's Person B or Person A; he knows for sure that his experiences of A's life come from the tea, and aren't his own. The uncertainty comes from whether Person B is really a separate real person, or merely Person C using the tea to experience B's life. C, in turn, isn't concerned of being A or B, but that there might be some D he doesn't know about that drunk tea made from C and now experiences his life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the tea would have had it's own designation before the loss of the pot. But that's just a matter of interpreting fictional SOP.

I cannot make heads or tails of that chart, which is a pity because I gather it outlines the whole mystery.

I like the existential uncertainty horror of the final note, it's one of my favorite flavors and Chen's characterization was very believable. But I'm not certain that the anomaly can support it. You drink the tea, you experience someone else's life, you come back to your own. I can see this being disorienting, even quite disturbing, and exponentially more-so if there are nested tea experiences. But unless it's a secondary effect, why would you ever doubt that you are back to the real you? You can be certain because you're memories of being person 2 ended when the tea was made, while your memories of being yourself bookend the recollections of p2's life. You now recall being you before you "were" p2, and you are now experiencing being you after the end of the p2 story.

On balance, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the table, but I can't quite get my head around why Davis is a prime suspect. He's verified to have had access and has no-one to back him up, but that's the same with several other researchers. Clearly I've missed or misinterpreted something, but I can't for the life of me work out what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, so the earlier dates are at the bottom of the timeline. I read it the other way around. Yeah, things make more sense now. So, it was the O5 who stole the skip. Neat. Well, that was my first impression but then I read your above post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's interesting, but I still have no idea how I'm meant to derive it from the timeline given. I simply don't understand what all the distinct shadings and the many, many uses of the word "ACCESS" actually indicate. It's just not a clear diagram at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cheers - re the lifespan, you're correct it's 'up to that point'. I think you're misreading the timeline - Fredericks drinks Jones's tea once about a week after it was created, which means he can't verify anything about a week before that point. On reviewing I did notice an issue with Dr Braddock seemingly verifying access by Chen after she provided a biopsy due to an artistically tapered shaded area, which I've since fixed.

Re the samples, this is the inventory taken after -1 was stolen; before that point they didn't classify the tea as -2 (see the archived Description). If you notice all addendums actually postdate the theft (see the Incident Report where it's mentioned that it's being written up at the conclusion six months later), so they've only inventoried extant samples.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As anqxyr points out, you only relive up to the point the tea is prepared, so you would relive your own life once (find out where you left those darn keys, except you would probably re-forget where you left them when you reached the point where you forgot them the first time, since you don't remember who you are and thus don't take differing actions from the first time around) :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks - the staff access timeline is fully worked-out, so you can use it to identify the prime suspects, namely Dr Braddock, Researcher Davis and Junior Researcher Nielsen - Nielsen reported the theft but could potentially have taken it himself during the blacked-out days. Dr Braddock has given affidavits for the character of three other staff members (Iglesias, Chen, and Fredericks through Chen) via -2 experiences, but is himself suspiciously unverifiable, and is in fact a reference to DrEverettMann's Tale 'Thank You For Your Time', which would make him, and not the poor guy who provided the 1416-2-12 sample, the MC&D plant in Sector-25. Also:

+ show block
– hide block

The staff access timeline is redacted because someone who appears to be an O5 and another individual used the skip the week after the Chen incident, which means at the time they thought the skip swapped consciousness between the drinker and the donor. This is probably unrelated to the theft, but the resulting coverup has made it extremely hard for the Foundation to work out whodunnit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I occurs to me I didn't comment on this one since I hadn't the time on first reading.

I'm really quite fond of it. This is a good twist on the body-swap trope, and the final note really pulls it together.

If I had a criticism of the piece, it would be the chart showing who accessed what and when. Though I like that you included such a clearly bureaucratic device into this SCP, I would like a legend or something to help me understand it. Right now it's very difficult to decipher, even with the short blurb. Also, the SCP-BLACKBOX references muddle it quite a bit.

Regardless, it's very good. I +1'd already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers - re 'duress' I've since changed the first instance to 'high-level interrogation' - I wanted to make clear that both Chen and D-3127 were subject to the same psychological torture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
human test subjects almost without exception claim to have experienced during this period the entire lifespan of the individual whose tissue was used to prepare the SCP-1416-2 sample

The entire lifespan up to the point the sample was taken, or the entire lifespan? I presume the former, but it isn't explicitly stated. Actually, the timeline implies the later, since Fredericks sees Jones' actions that happened after Fredericks drunk the tea. But if that's the case, Chen's statement that "the only way to be sure is to see them die" is incorrect, since you can still be them drinking the tea at some earlier point before their death. Uh. Maybe I'm reading the timeline wrong.

zaratustra beat me to recursion.

The notes imply multiple in-between samples taken, but the numbering of the preparations is consecutive.

The timeline is neat. It would be better if the skip's number wasn't blacked out, and it implies researchers drinking each other's tea repeatedly, which is a little bit lolFoundationy, but I still like it as is.

I don't like the Chen's note, it seems too overt and hysterical. It's not like living someone else's life somehow alters or invalidates it. Oh well, looks like I'm in the minority here.

As a whole, this is good, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WB9tR1NunqU&desktop_uri=%2Fwatch%3Fv%3DWB9tR1NunqU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a comic called Chew where the main character is a cibopath, who can take a bite of something and get a psychic impression from it. This is much more disturbing. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first test says that there was a tissue sample in the thing when they first found it. Presumably they noticed that it had been brewed and decided to re-test it using the original material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How in the world did they figure out you should make human tea using the thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if Xzibit or Inception joke is better here. Xzibitception? Otherwise, I thought this article was dry right up until the experiment at the end, and I'm gonna have to mull it over whether that's good enough for me for an upvote. Definitely at least neutral, leaning positive about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You relive reliving yourself reliving yourself reliving yourself while reliving yourself, and during which, you relive yourself reliving yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if someone eats their own sample?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like most of this (and have upvoted it accordingly), but I have to say I don't really understand the addendum with the timelines of staff access, or what it's trying to tell me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done, SRegan. I felt that the preparation log of the samples was unnerving and captured the tone of the Foundation well, and Chen's statement really leaves the reader with a lot to think over.

Upvoted without hesitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like how the whole "switching bodies" idea is done here. Though I was a bit hesitant at first, the addendums certainly pulled this together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is…an odd one. It's undoubtedly well written, but there's just something about it that rubbed me the wrong way. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A simple idea with a great plot. Well done!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The graphic at the end made my mind. Another reason for my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this okay until I reached Chen's statement. That's when I loved it. That's creepy on a level we don't see around here often. The idea you could make a sort of Russian nesting doll experience, someone reliving anothers life, then you reliving that person reliving that life, so on and so forth, is just…damn. I don't even know why that's so unsettling, but it is. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really well-written, and takes what could have been an otherwise unexceptional idea to new levels. This kind of bothered me, though:

very high levels of duress that were not present in Junior Researcher Chen under the same levels of duress.

I'm not sure if one of the two uses of 'duress' was meant to be something else, but it seems kind of circular and awkward as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First a ceramic vessel, then samples of a substance… Creepy… +1
